text.title: competitive-intelligence Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for competitive-intelligence. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Monitor each signal category at its natural change rate: pricing and funding weekly, feature launches and website changes daily, hiring and content/SEO monthly.
rule.rationale: Polling too slowly misses time-sensitive funding and pricing signals. Polling too frequently on slow-moving signals wastes quota and generates noise.

rule.statement: Pick a platform tier matched to your scale: Crayon/Klue for mid-market automation and battlecard distribution; Contify Athena for agentic unstructured-content analysis; AlphaSense for filings and expert-call transcripts.
rule.rationale: Over-engineering CI infrastructure before you have 3+ named competitors and an action loop wastes budget. Under-investing means alerts are never acted on.

rule.statement: Build toward predictive CI by feeding historical event logs into a threat-scoring model calibrated quarterly against actual lost deals.
rule.rationale: Descriptive CI is table stakes. Predictive CI compresses the gap between a competitor signal and your strategic response from weeks to hours.

rule.statement: Stamp every battlecard with a generation date and enforce a 14-day TTL; a 2-week-old battlecard with confident tone is worse than no battlecard.
rule.rationale: Sales reps trust dated artifacts. Stale-but-confident claims actively damage deals.

rule.statement: Assign Haiku to mechanical collection and classification; Sonnet to synthesis and battlecard writing; Opus to threat assessment and scenario planning—never invert this.
rule.rationale: Mixing reasoning-grade models into collection tasks adds 10x cost with no marginal accuracy gain. Mixing fast models into threat analysis produces hallucinated strategic claims.

rule.statement: Every digest and battlecard must pass a fact-checker agent that HEAD-requests every cited URL before the document is distributed.
rule.rationale: LLMs invent URLs like /pricing/enterprise that never existed. Publishing an invented citation to a sales rep destroys trust in the entire CI function.

rule.statement: Emit one event per delta, never aggregate inside the collector—keeps the audit trail clean and lets the synthesizer see the raw timeline.
rule.rationale: Aggregated events hide the sequence of competitor moves. The synthesizer needs the timeline to detect acceleration patterns.

rule.statement: Force every LLM claim to carry an event_id reference; reject digests that fail the fact-checker pass.
rule.rationale: Unsourced claims are liabilities in sales conversations. Require provenance.

rule.statement: Calibrate threat scores against actual lost deals quarterly; recalibrate the prompt if the score does not predict losses.
rule.rationale: An uncalibrated threat score becomes a noise source. Quarterly calibration ties CI to revenue impact.

rule.statement: Battlecard TTL ≤ 14 days. Stamp generation date prominently; sales reps trust dated artifacts.
rule.rationale: Stale battlecards with confident tone damage credibility. A 14-day TTL enforces freshness.

rule.statement: Separate "what changed" (Haiku, mechanical) from "so what" (Opus, judgment). Mixing them costs 10x and adds hallucination.
rule.rationale: Opus reasoning on raw HTML diffs is wasteful and noisy. Haiku distills the signal; Opus interprets the pattern.

rule.statement: Subscribe to competitor changelog/RSS first; scraping is the fallback. APIs > RSS > scrape > screenshot diff.
rule.rationale: APIs are reliable and ToS-compliant. Scraping is fragile and legally ambiguous. Use the most reliable source available per competitor.

rule.statement: Track yourself with the same pipeline. Catches what your own marketing site exposes to competitors.
rule.rationale: CI is bidirectional. What you publish about pricing, hiring, and product reveals your strategy to the same tools your competitors use.

rule.statement: Keep a "do-not-track" list (legal, ex-employees, personal blogs) to avoid ToS and ethical issues.
rule.rationale: Scraping without guardrails creates legal and reputational risk.

rule.statement: Run win/loss interviews monthly and feed transcripts back into battlecard prompts—review-site sentiment alone is a lagging indicator.
rule.rationale: Win/loss data grounds CI in real deal outcomes, not sentiment signals.
